title: Introduction to the Immune System
section: The Immune System in Health and Disease

items.subtopic: Introduction to the Immune System | DEFINITIONS (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Adaptive Immunity: Role of T and B Lymphocytes
items.content: The adaptive immune system is a sophisticated defense mechanism mediated by T and B lymphocytes, which recognize specific antigens through clonotypic receptors. These receptors are products of gene rearrangements that occur during development and throughout the organism's life.
keyPoints: Adaptive immunity involves T and B lymphocytes.
Immune responses rely on antigen recognition by clonotypic receptors.
Receptors result from gene rearrangement during development.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · Introduction to the Immune System | DEFINITIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Introduction to the Immune System | DEFINITIONS (part 1)
items.title: Antibodies: Central Components of B-Cell Receptor
items.content: Antibodies are molecules produced by B cells, encoded by genes that rearrange during development. They consist of immunoglobulin heavy and light chains forming the central component of the B-cell receptor (BCR) for antigen.
keyPoints: Antibodies are produced by B cells.
They consist of immunoglobulin heavy and light chains.
Antibodies form the central part of the BCR.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · Introduction to the Immune System | DEFINITIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Introduction to the Immune System | DEFINITIONS (part 1)
items.title: Autoimmune vs. Autoinflammatory Diseases: Key Differences
items.content: Autoimmune diseases involve overreactive adaptive immune cells like autoreactive T and B cells, leading to pathogenic responses. In contrast, autoinflammatory diseases are hereditary disorders characterized by innate inflammatory response mutations without evidence of adaptive immune cell activation.
keyPoints: Autoimmune diseases involve autoreactive T and B cells.
Autoinflammatory diseases result from innate immune system mutations.
No adaptive immune cell activation in autoinflammatory diseases.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · Introduction to the Immune System | DEFINITIONS (part 1)

items.title: Role of Fc Receptors in Antibody-Dependent Cell-Mediated Cytotoxicity
items.content: Fc receptors on immune cells bind to the Fc domains of antibodies attached to pathogen-infected cells, facilitating antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC). This mechanism is crucial for the destruction of infected cells by cytotoxic cells.
keyPoints: Fc receptors are found on B cells, NK cells, macrophages, neutrophils, and mast cells.
Examples include CD16 (FcγRIIa), CD23 (FcεR), CD32 (FcγRII), CD64 (FcγRI), and CD89 (FcαR).
ADCC is a key immune response mechanism.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · DEFINITIONS (part 2)

items.title: Inflammasome Activation and Its Role in Innate Immunity
items.content: The inflammasome is a cytoplasmic complex that activates IL-1β and IL-18 cytokines, linking microbial sensing to inflammatory responses. This activation is vital for the innate immune system's response to intracellular pathogens.
keyPoints: Inflammasomes link microbial product sensing to IL-1β and IL-18 activation.
Crucial for innate immune response to intracellular pathogens.
Involved in both health and pathological states.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · DEFINITIONS (part 2)

items.title: Characteristics of Natural Killer (NK) Cells
items.content: NK cells are a type of innate lymphoid cell that target cells with low or absent HLA class I molecules, such as cancerous or virally infected cells. They express receptors that inhibit their function when self-MHC class I is present.
keyPoints: NK cells kill targets with few/no HLA class I molecules.
Express inhibitory receptors for self-MHC class I.
Functionally analogous to CD8 cytotoxic T cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · DEFINITIONS (part 2)

items.title: Role of T_H17 Cells in Immune Response
items.content: T_H17 cells are a subset of CD4 T cells that secrete cytokines IL-17, IL-22, and IL-26. They play dual roles in defending against bacterial and fungal pathogens while also being implicated in autoimmune inflammatory disorders.
keyPoints: T_H17 cells are regulated by the transcription factor ROR γt.
They secrete IL-17, IL-22, and IL-26.
Involved in both defense against pathogens and autoimmune inflammation.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · DEFINITIONS (part 3) | INTRODUCTION

items.title: Mechanisms of Immune Tolerance
items.content: Tolerance is the nonresponsiveness of B- and T-cells to antigens, achieved through central or peripheral mechanisms. Central tolerance involves deletion in primary lymphoid organs, while peripheral tolerance includes cell deletion or anergy.
keyPoints: Central tolerance occurs via B-cell deletion in bone marrow or T-cell deletion in the thymus.
Peripheral tolerance is maintained by cell deletion or inducing anergy.
Tolerance prevents immune responses to self-antigens and nonpathogenic microbes.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · DEFINITIONS (part 3) | INTRODUCTION

items.subtopic: THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION
items.title: Role of Pattern Recognition Receptors (PRRs) in Innate Immunity
items.content: Pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) are crucial for detecting pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) and danger signals, leading to immune activation. They include transmembrane proteins like Toll-like receptors (TLRs) and cytoplasmic proteins such as NOD-like receptors (NLRs).
keyPoints: PRRs recognize PAMPs and host cell molecules produced in response to infection.
Major families of PRRs: TLRs, CLRs, RLRs, NLRs, collectins like MBL, pentraxins, macrophage scavenger receptors, and integrins.
TLRs are expressed on various cells including macrophages, dendritic cells (DCs), B cells, and nonhematopoietic cell types; 11 TLRs identified in humans.
Signaling through TLR4 by LPS can lead to cytokine release and LPS-induced shock. Mutations in TLR4 can protect against this.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION

items.subtopic: THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION
items.title: Inflammasomes: Intracellular Sensors Triggering Inflammation
items.content: NOD-like receptors (NLRs) form inflammasomes, which are cytoplasmic complexes that activate inflammatory caspases and IL-1β in response to PAMPs and nonbacterial danger signals.
keyPoints: Inflammasomes consist of NLRP proteins and activate upon sensing PAMPs or cell stress.
Mutations in inflammasome components can lead to autoinflammatory syndromes.
Polymorphisms in inflammasome components affect susceptibility to infections, autoimmune diseases, and crystallopathies like gout.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION

items.subtopic: THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION
items.title: Connection Between Innate and Adaptive Immune Systems via PRRs
items.content: PRRs facilitate communication between the innate and adaptive immune systems, with TLRs playing a key role in cytokine production and upregulation of cell-surface molecules for antigen presentation.
keyPoints: LPS-binding protein transfers LPS to CD14 on macrophages, linking innate and adaptive responses.
TLRs bind LPS and signal immune cells to produce cytokines and initiate adaptive immunity.
Mutations in TLRs can protect against inflammatory diseases like LPS-induced asthma.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION

items.title: Role of Monocytes-Macrophages in Innate Immunity
items.content: Monocytes-macrophages are crucial for the innate immune response, acting as first-line defenders by ingesting and destroying microorganisms. They release toxic products like hydrogen peroxide and nitric oxide to kill pathogens.
keyPoints: Monocytes circulate with a half-life of 1-3 days before migrating into tissues.

Macrophages produce inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins, leukotrienes, platelet activating factor, cytokines (IL-1, TNF-α, IL-6, IL-12), and chemokines to recruit additional immune cells like neutrophils.
Monocytes-macrophages express receptors for IgG Fc region, activated complement components, and various cytokines.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: Dendritic Cells as Potent Antigen-Presenting Cells
items.content: Dendritic cells are the most potent classical antigen-presenting cells, bridging innate and adaptive immunity by presenting antigens to T cells.
keyPoints: Human dendritic cells include myeloid DCs and plasmacytoid DCs.
Plasmacytoid DCs produce type I interferon (IFN-α) in response to viral infections, activating NK cells and CD8 T cells.
Dendritic cells upregulate MHC class II, B7-1 (CD80), and B7-2 (CD86) upon pathogen contact, enhancing antigen presentation.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: Dendritic Cells in Immune Response Modulation
items.content: Dendritic cells modulate immune responses by determining the type and quality of adaptive immunity through TLR expression and activation.
keyPoints: TLR engagement on DCs triggers cytokine production, activating innate immune cells and recruiting adaptive immune cells.
Different TLRs (e.g., TLR7-9 in plasmacytoid DCs, TLR4 in myeloid DCs) influence the nature of the immune response.
Dendritic cells attract various immune cells including helper and cytotoxic T cells, B cells, and regulatory T cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: NK Cells: Mechanisms of Cytotoxicity
items.content: NK cells are innate analogues to CD8 cytotoxic T cells, mediating granzyme and perforin-based cytotoxic activity. They express Fc receptors for antibody-mediated ADCC and possess a variety of surface receptors that regulate their activation and inhibition.
keyPoints: NK cells produce IFN-γ and depend on the transcription factor T-bet.
NK cell cytotoxicity is MHC-unrestricted, targeting malignant or virus-infected cells.
NK cells mediate ADCC via Fc receptor (CD16) binding to opsonized targets.
KIRs regulate NK activity, with inhibitory KIRDL and activating KIRDS based on cytoplasmic tail length.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 2)

items.title: ILC3s in Gut Immunity
items.content: ILC3s, dependent on RORγt, produce IL-17 and are critical for immune responses against extracellular pathogens. They also play a role in maintaining gut homeostasis by promoting Treg differentiation to suppress unnecessary immune reactions.
keyPoints: ILC3s mirror CD4 Tₘ₁₇ lymphocytes and respond to bacteria and fungi.
A subset of RORγt+ ILCs may stimulate naïve CD4 T cells into peripheral Tregs in the gut.
Imbalance in immune response to microbiome can contribute to inflammatory bowel diseases.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 2)

items.title: NK Cell Receptor Diversity and Function
items.content: NK cells express a variety of receptors, including KIRs and NCRs, which mediate their activation or inhibition. These receptors play crucial roles in immune surveillance by distinguishing between healthy self-cells and malignant or infected targets.
keyPoints: KIRs have HLA class I ligands and include inhibitory (KIRDL) and activating (KIRDS) types.
NCRs, such as NKp46, NKp30, and NKp44, recognize pathogen molecules on target cells.
NK cell signaling involves a balance of inhibitory and activating signals to prevent damage to healthy cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 2)

items.title: NK T Cells: Bridging Innate and Adaptive Immunity
items.content: NK T cells, expressing CD3 and invariant TCR-α chains, recognize lipid molecules from intracellular bacteria like Listeria monocytogenes and Mycobacterium tuberculosis when presented by CD1 on APCs. This recognition triggers cytokine secretion (IL-4, IFN-γ), activating dendritic cells and serving as a crucial innate defense.
keyPoints: NK T cells express CD3 and invariant TCR-α chains.
Recognize lipid molecules from intracellular bacteria via CD1 on APCs.
Secrete IL-4 and IFN-γ upon activation.
Activate dendritic cells, aiding in defense against Listeria monocytogenes and Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 3)

items.title: Role of Fcγ Receptors in Immune Regulation
items.content: Fcγ receptors on NK cells, B cells, macrophages, neutrophils, and mast cells mediate IgG interactions with antibody-coated targets. Activation FcRs have ITAM sequences, while inhibitory ones have ITIM sequences. Dysregulation of these interactions is implicated in diseases like arthritis, multiple sclerosis, and systemic lupus erythematosus.
keyPoints: Fcγ receptors present on NK cells, B cells, macrophages, neutrophils, mast cells.
Activation FcRs (e.g., FcγRI, FcγRIIa) have ITAM sequences; inhibitory FcRs (e.g., FcγRIIb) have ITIM sequences.
Dysregulation linked to arthritis, multiple sclerosis, systemic lupus erythematosus.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 3)

items.title: Granulocytes: Key Players in Inflammation and Immune Response
items.content: Neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils are granulocytes involved in inflammation. Neutrophils release enzymes and superoxide radicals causing tissue damage. Eosinophils target parasitic infections with cytotoxic proteins but also have anti-inflammatory roles. Basophils and mast cells produce cytokines and mediate allergic responses.
keyPoints: Granulocytes include neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils.
Neutrophils release enzymes and superoxide radicals causing tissue damage.
Eosinophils target parasites with cytotoxic proteins; also have anti-inflammatory roles.
Basophils/mast cells produce cytokines and mediate allergic responses.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 3)

items.title: Complement System Pathways and Their Activation
items.content: The complement system is a crucial part of innate immunity, consisting of plasma enzymes and regulatory proteins that activate in a cascade to lyse cells. There are four pathways: classic, MBL, alternative, and terminal. The classic pathway is triggered by antigen/antibody complexes, the MBL pathway by microbes with mannose groups, and the alternative pathway by microbes or tumor cells directly binding C3.
keyPoints: Complement system includes plasma enzymes and regulatory proteins activated in a cascade.

Four pathways: classic (activated by immune complexes), MBL (activated by microbes with terminal mannose), alternative (activated by direct pathogen binding to C3), and terminal pathway leading to cell lysis.
Classic pathway links innate and adaptive immunity via antibody in immune complexes.
Alternative pathway is antibody-independent, activated directly by pathogens or tumor cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 4)

items.title: Functional Consequences of Complement Activation
items.content: Complement activation through its pathways results in several functional outcomes: facilitating microbe or damaged cell clearance, promoting inflammation, and causing lysis of microbes or opsonized cells. The cleavage products of complement components play roles in these processes.
keyPoints: Cleavage products facilitate microbe or damaged cell clearance (C1q, C4, C3).
Promote activation and enhancement of inflammation through anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a).
Promote lysis of microbes or opsonized cells via the membrane attack complex.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · EFFECTOR CELLS OF INNATE IMMUNITY (part 4)

items.title: Cytokine Families and Receptor Structures
items.content: Cytokines are categorized into three major structural families: the hematopoietic family, the TNF/IL-1/PDGF/TGF-$\beta$ families, and the CXC/C-C chemokine families. Their receptors can be grouped into five general families based on extracellular amino acid sequences and conserved structural domains.
keyPoints: Cytokines belong to three major structural families: hematopoietic family; TNF/IL-1/PDGF/TGF-$\beta$ families; CXC/C-C chemokine families.
Chemokines regulate cell movement and act through G protein-coupled receptors with a distinctive structure.
Cytokine receptors are grouped into five families based on extracellular amino acid sequences and conserved structural domains.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CYTOKINES (part 1)

items.title: Functionality of Cytokines in Immune Responses
items.content: Cytokines influence gene activation leading to cellular functions such as growth, differentiation, and effector function. They are pivotal in immune response regulation, with T cells categorized by cytokine secretion patterns into $T_H1$, $T_H2$, and $T_H17$ types.
keyPoints: Cytokines influence gene activation resulting in cellular activation, growth, differentiation, and effector function.
$T_H1$ cells are associated with cell-mediated immune response; $T_H2$ cells with humoral immune response; $T_H17$ cells defend against extracellular bacteria and fungi at mucosal sites.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CYTOKINES (part 1)

items.title: Cytokine Receptor Families and Signal Transduction
items.content: The cytokine receptor families include the hematopoietic growth factor receptors, interferon receptors, and TNF receptors. Specific subunits like gp130, a common 150-kDa subunit, and $\gamma_c$ are crucial for signal transduction.
keyPoints: Hematopoietic growth factor receptor family has conserved motifs: N-terminal cysteine-rich region and C-terminal WSXWS motif.
Cytokines like IL-6, IL-11, IL-12 share gp130; IL-3, IL-5, GM-CSF share a 150-kDa subunit; IL-2, IL-4, IL-7, IL-9, IL-15 share $\gamma_c$.
Mutations in the $\gamma_c$ gene result in X-linked severe combined immune deficiency syndrome (X-SCID).
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CYTOKINES (part 1)

items.title: HIV-1 Entry Mechanism via Chemokine Receptors
items.content: The chemokine receptors CXCR4 and CCR5 serve as major co-receptors for HIV-1 entry into CD4-expressing host cells. Understanding this mechanism is crucial for targeting therapeutic interventions.
keyPoints: CXCR4 and CCR5 are key co-receptors for HIV-1 binding and entry.
This knowledge aids in the development of antiretroviral therapies targeting these receptors.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CYTOKINES (part 2)

items.title: JAK-STAT Pathway in Lymphoid Development
items.content: The JAK-STAT signaling pathway is essential for lymphoid development, with different STATs binding specific receptor subunits and regulating gene expression. Mutations in JAK3 result in X-linked SCID (X-SCID) phenotype.
keyPoints: JAK kinases include JAK1, JAK2, JAK3, and Tyk2, each binding different cytokine receptors.
STATs bind phosphorylated receptors via SH2 domains and regulate gene expression by translocating to the nucleus.
Mutations in JAK3 cause a disorder identical to X-SCID, affecting both boys and girls as it is on chromosome 19.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CYTOKINES (part 2)

items.title: Adaptive Immunity: Mechanisms and Memory
items.content: Adaptive immunity is characterized by antigen-specific responses and immunologic memory, which results in more rapid and vigorous immune responses upon subsequent exposures to the same antigen.
keyPoints: Adaptive immunity involves cellular (T lymphocytes) and humoral (B lymphocytes) components.
Immunologic priming leads to faster and stronger responses on re-exposure due to immunologic memory.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.title: T-Cell Maturation and Distribution
items.content: T-cell precursors mature in the thymus, with a daily export of ~2% of total thymocytes. The distribution of T cells varies across different tissues: 70–80% in peripheral blood lymphocytes, 90% in thoracic duct lymphocytes, 30-40% in lymph node cells, and 20-30% in spleen lymphoid cells.
keyPoints: T-cell precursors mature in the thymus with a daily emigration of ~2% of total thymocytes.
Distribution: 70–80% in peripheral blood lymphocytes, 90% in thoracic duct lymphocytes, 30-40% in lymph node cells, and 20-30% in spleen lymphoid cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.title: Role of CD4+ T Cells
items.content: CD4+ T cells are crucial for regulating the function of T and B lymphocytes, monocytes, and erythroid cell maturation. They produce cytokines and engage in direct cell contact to activate B cells and induce immunoglobulin isotype switching.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cells regulate T and B lymphocyte and monocyte functions via cytokine production and direct cell contact.
They play a role in erythroid cell maturation and B-cell activation through CD40 ligand interaction.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.title: Mechanism of T-cell Selection and Maturation
items.content: T-cell selection in the thymus involves positive and negative selection processes that shape the T-cell repertoire. Positive selection activates thymocytes with low-affinity interactions between their TCRs and self-peptides presented by MHC molecules, allowing them to survive and mature into CD4+ or CD8+ single-positive T cells. Negative selection eliminates thymocytes with high-affinity interactions to prevent autoimmunity.
keyPoints: Positive selection: Thymocytes with low-affinity TCRs for self-MHC-peptide complexes survive.
Negative selection: Thymocytes with high-affinity TCRs undergo apoptosis.
Outcome: Elimination of autoreactive T cells and maturation into functional MHC-restricted T cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.title: Molecular Basis of T-cell Antigen Recognition
items.content: T-cell receptors (TCRs) are composed of a heterodimer of αβ or γδ chains linked with CD3 subunits, which transduce activation signals. The genes encoding TCRs undergo rearrangement during maturation to create diversity in antigen recognition.
keyPoints: TCR structure: Heterodimer (αβ or γδ) linked with CD3 subunits.
Gene rearrangement: V(D)J recombination for α, β, γ, δ chains on chromosomes 14 and 7.
Diversity generation: Through gene segment permutations, N-region diversification, and chain pairing.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.title: T-cell Recognition of Antigen Peptides
items.content: TCR-αβ cells recognize short peptide fragments (~9–13 amino acids) derived from protein antigens presented by antigen-presenting cells, not native proteins or carbohydrates.
keyPoints: Recognition: TCRs bind to 9–13 amino acid peptides.
Antigen presentation: Peptides are processed and presented by MHC molecules on APCs.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism of Antigen Presentation by MHC Class I and II
items.content: Antigens are processed and presented to T cells via distinct pathways involving MHC class I or II molecules. Exogenous antigens are degraded into peptides, associated with MHC class II, and recognized by CD4+ T cells. Endogenous antigens are processed in the cytosol, transported into the endoplasmic reticulum, and presented on MHC class I to CD8+ T cells.
keyPoints: Exogenous antigens use the exogenous antigen-presentation pathway with MHC class II molecules.
Endogenous antigens are processed by proteasomes and transported via TAP proteins for presentation on MHC class I.
CD4 stabilizes interactions between TCR and MHC class II; CD8 does so for MHC class I.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 3)

items.title: Presentation of Lipid Antigens by CD1 Molecules
items.content: Lipids from intracellular bacteria like M. tuberculosis are presented to T cells via MHC-related CD1 molecules, not MHC class I or II. This allows recognition by subsets of $\gamma\delta$ and CD8+ TCR-$\alpha\beta$ T cells, contributing to innate immunity.
keyPoints: Bacterial lipid antigens are presented in the context of CD1 molecules.
Some $\gamma\delta$ T cells recognize these lipids without prior antigen priming.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 3)

items.title: T-Cell Activation and Immunologic Synapse Formation
items.content: Upon engagement of the TCR by a foreign peptide-MHC complex, adhesion molecules like CD54-CD11/18 and CD58-CD2 stabilize the interaction. This leads to the formation of lipid rafts or immunologic synapses that concentrate signaling molecules essential for T-cell activation.
keyPoints: Adhesion ligands such as CD54-CD11/18 and CD58-CD2 are critical for stabilizing MHC-peptide-TCR binding.
Lipid rafts coalesce key signaling molecules like the TCR/CD3 complex, ZAP-70, and LAT during activation.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 3)

items.title: Mechanisms of T-Cell Activation
items.content: T-cell activation involves the stabilization of TCR-MHC binding, leading to signaling through ITAMs on CD3 chains. This results in the activation of transcription factors like NF-AT, fos and jun, and rel/NF-κB, which induce cytokine gene expression.
keyPoints: TCR does not have intrinsic signaling activity but uses ITAMs for signal transduction.
Activation pathways include src family PTKs (Fyn, Lck), ZAP-70, Syk, calcineurin, ras, and protein kinase C pathways.
Transcription factors activated: NF-AT, fos/jun, rel/NF-κB.
Induces expression of IL-2, IL-2 receptor, IL-4, TNF-α.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 4)

items.title: T-Cell Exhaustion in Chronic Infections and Cancer
items.content: Chronic antigen presence leads to T-cell exhaustion, characterized by reduced proliferation and effector molecule production. PD-1 and CTLA-4 are key markers of this state, with therapeutic strategies targeting their inhibition.
keyPoints: T-cell exhaustion occurs in chronic viral infections (HIV-1, hepatitis C/B) and malignancies.
Associated with high expression of PD-1 (CD279).
Exhausted T cells show reduced IL-2, TNF-α, IFN-γ production.
Inhibition of PD-1 or CTLA-4 is a therapeutic strategy for enhancing anti-tumor responses.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 4)

items.title: Superantigen Effects on T Cells
items.content: Superantigens bind directly to the lateral portion of the TCR-β chain and MHC class II β chain, activating a large proportion of T cells. This can lead to excessive cytokine production and conditions like toxic shock syndrome.
keyPoints: Superantigens activate up to 20% of peripheral T cells.
Conventional antigens activate <1 in 10,000 T cells.
Examples include staphylococcal enterotoxins.
Can cause toxic shock syndrome with massive cytokine overproduction.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 4)

items.title: Mechanisms of B-cell Maturation and Diversity
items.content: B lymphocyte development involves antigen-independent and antigen-dependent phases. Antigen-independent maturation occurs in primary lymphoid organs, leading to sIg+ mature B cells. Antigen-dependent maturation happens in secondary lymphoid organs and includes processes like memory B-cell induction, Ig class switching, and plasma cell formation. Diversity of the antigen-binding variable region is generated through ordered gene rearrangements and somatic hypermutation.
keyPoints: B-cell development occurs in two phases: antigen-independent (primary lymphoid organs) and antigen-dependent (secondary lymphoid organs).
Diversity is achieved via Ig gene rearrangement and somatic hypermutation, leading to a vast array of antibody specificities.
~300 Vκ genes and 5 Jκ genes create >1500 kappa light chain combinations; ~70 Vλ genes and 4 Jλ genes produce >280 lambda light chain combinations.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 5)

items.title: Regulation of Ig Gene Rearrangement
items.content: Ig gene rearrangement is a tightly regulated process ensuring each B cell produces a single antibody specificity. This involves allelic exclusion, where only one light chain and one heavy chain gene are productively rearranged per B cell. The pre-BCR signals proliferation and maturation of early B-cell precursors in the bone marrow.
keyPoints: Ig gene rearrangement results in single antibody specificity per B cell through allelic exclusion.
The pre-BCR, composed of μ heavy chains and a surrogate light chain (Vpre-B), drives early B-cell development.
IL-7 from bone marrow stroma is crucial for the proliferation and maturation of pro- and pre-B cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 5)

items.title: Mechanisms to Prevent Autoimmunity in B Cells
items.content: Random Ig gene rearrangements can lead to self-reactive antibodies. Mechanisms like BCR editing and negative selection are crucial for preventing autoimmunity. BCR editing attempts to mutate autoreactive receptors, while unsuccessful cases may undergo apoptosis through negative selection.
keyPoints: BCR editing mutates autoreactive BCRs to prevent self-reactivity.
Negative selection induces apoptosis in autoreactive B cells if receptor editing fails.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 5)

items.title: Mechanism and Importance of Affinity Maturation in B Cells
items.content: Affinity maturation is a process where somatic hypermutation occurs, leading to the production of high-affinity antibodies by memory B cells. This process takes place after B cells encounter foreign antigens that react with their BCRs.
keyPoints: Somatic hypermutation involves point mutations in H- and L-genes, resulting in mutant sIg molecules with potentially higher antigen affinity.
Affinity maturation ensures the generation of antibodies with the best binding capabilities.
Memory B cells in peripheral lymph organs undergo this process to produce high-affinity antibodies.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 6)

items.title: Role and Mechanism of Ig Class Switching
items.content: Ig class switching is a critical mechanism in adaptive immunity, allowing B cells to produce different antibody isotypes. This process is driven by interactions between CD40 on B cells and CD40 ligand on T cells, along with cytokines like IL-4 and TGF-$ \beta $.
keyPoints: Ig class switching occurs in germinal centers of lymphoid tissues.
CD40 and CD40L interaction is essential for driving Ig class switching.
Cytokines such as IL-1, -2, -4, -5, and -6 promote B cell proliferation and differentiation into Ig-secreting cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 6)

items.title: Clinical Relevance of IgG Subclasses
items.content: IgG constitutes the majority of serum immunoglobulins, with its subclasses having distinct roles and clinical implications. Variations in their levels can influence susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: IgG makes up 75–85% of total serum immunoglobulin.
The four IgG subclasses are ordered by serum level: IgG1 > IgG2 > IgG3 > IgG4.
Deficiencies in specific IgG subclasses can lead to increased susceptibility to bacterial infections.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 6)

items.title: Role of IgM in Immune Responses and Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: IgM is the first antibody to appear during an immune response, functioning as a primary responder. It circulates as a pentamer and plays a critical role in forming immune complexes, particularly in autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis.
keyPoints: IgM appears first in the primary antibody response.
Circulates as a 950-kDa pentamer with bivalent monomers joined by the J chain.
Functions as an antigen receptor on mature B cells.
Involved in immune complexes in autoimmune diseases, e.g., rheumatoid factors in rheumatoid arthritis.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 7)

items.title: Function and Distribution of IgA
items.content: IgA is the predominant immunoglobulin in secretions, playing a crucial role in mucosal immunity by preventing viral binding to epithelial cells. It exists as secretory IgA (sIgA) in secretions.
keyPoints: Constitutes 7–15% of total serum immunoglobulin but is predominant in secretions.
Secretory IgA consists of two IgA monomers, a J chain, and a secretory protein.
IgA1 is primarily found in serum; IgA2 is more prevalent in secretions.
Fixes complement via the alternative pathway and has antiviral activity by preventing virus binding to epithelial cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · THE ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 7)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 1)
items.title: Role of T_H1 Cells in Intracellular Pathogen Defense
items.content: T_H1 CD4+ T cells are crucial for mediating intracellular killing by pathogens, primarily through the production of IFN-γ. They assist in generating cytotoxic T cells and opsonizing antibodies, responding to antigens that cause delayed hypersensitivity reactions.
keyPoints: T_H1 cells produce IFN-γ.
They help generate cytotoxic T cells and some types of opsonizing antibody.
Respond to intracellular pathogens like HIV-1 or M. tuberculosis.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 1)
items.title: Function of T_H2 Cells in Humoral Immunity
items.content: T_H2 cells primarily regulate humoral immunity and isotype switching, producing IL-4 and IL-10 to limit proinflammatory responses by T_H1 cells. They assist B cells in specific Ig production, responding to antigens requiring high antibody levels for elimination.
keyPoints: T_H2 cells produce IL-4 and IL-10.
They regulate humoral immunity and isotype switching.
Provide help to B cells for specific Ig production.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 1)
items.title: T_H17 Cells in Autoimmune Inflammatory Disorders
items.content: T_H17 cells secrete cytokines IL-17, -22, and -26, playing roles in autoimmune inflammatory disorders and defense against extracellular bacteria and fungi at mucosal surfaces.
keyPoints: T_H17 cells secrete IL-17, -22, and -26.
Involved in autoimmune inflammatory disorders.
Defend against extracellular bacteria and fungi.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 2)
items.title: Role of Treg Cells in Immune Regulation and Autoimmunity
items.content: T regulatory cells (Tregs), expressing CD25 and producing IL-10, are crucial for maintaining immune tolerance to self-antigens. Their loss is linked to organ-specific autoimmune diseases such as autoimmune thyroiditis, adrenalitis, oophoritis, and inflammatory bowel disease.
keyPoints: Treg cells express the α chain of the IL-2 receptor (CD25) and produce IL-10.
They suppress T- and B-cell responses.
Loss of Tregs causes organ-specific autoimmune diseases in mice: autoimmune thyroiditis, adrenalitis, oophoritis.
Tregs are involved in inflammatory bowel disease.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 2)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 2)
items.title: Mechanism of B-Cell Isotype Switching and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: B cells require T-cell help for high-affinity antibody production, involving antigen processing by B cells, CD40 ligand interaction, and cytokine induction. Mutations in the T-cell CD40 ligand result in X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome due to impaired isotype switching.
keyPoints: B cells need T-cell help for high-affinity antibody production of multiple isotypes.
CD40 ligand expression by activated T cells is critical for B-cell isotype switching.
Mutations in the T-cell CD40 ligand lead to X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome, characterized by lack of memory B-cell generation and inability to undergo isotype switching.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CELLULAR INTERACTIONS IN REGULATION OF NORMAL IMMUNE RESPONSES (part 2)

items.title: Mechanisms of Immune Tolerance
items.content: Immune tolerance involves mechanisms that prevent pathogenic autoreactivity to self-antigens, classified as cell intrinsic or extrinsic. Cell intrinsic mechanisms include apoptosis and anergy, while cell extrinsic mechanisms involve suppression by regulatory cells like Tregs.
keyPoints: Cell intrinsic mechanisms: apoptosis and induction of cell unresponsiveness (anergy).
Cell extrinsic mechanisms: suppression by immunomodulatory cells such as Tregs.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: Autoimmune Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Autoimmune diseases arise from the activation of autoreactive T or B cells without other causes like infections. Factors include genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, infectious agents, and loss of regulatory T cells.
keyPoints: Genetic factors: HLA-B27 associated with ankylosing spondylitis.
Environmental triggers: drugs like procainamide and phenytoin linked to systemic lupus erythematosus.
Infectious agents: Epstein-Barr virus can trigger autoantibody production against red blood cells and platelets.
Loss of Treg cells leading to conditions such as thyroiditis, adrenalitis, and oophoritis.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.title: Role and Function of Mucosa-Associated Lymphoid Tissue (MALT)
items.content: MALT is crucial for mucosal immunity, protecting against pathogens while preventing immune responses to benign antigens. It functions independently from systemic immune organs and includes specialized cells like M cells and ILCs.
keyPoints: MALT contains 80% of all immune cells in the body.
Functions: protection from pathogens, prevention of uptake of foreign antigens, and prevention of pathologic responses.
Secretory IgA production: >50 mg/kg body weight per 24 h, inhibits bacterial adhesion, neutralizes viruses.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 1)

items.subtopic: IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | THE CELLULAR AND MOLECULAR CONTROL OF PROGRAMMED CELL DEATH
items.title: Impact of Gut Microbiome on Immune System Function and Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: The gut microbiome plays a crucial role in maintaining immune system balance. Disruption of normal commensal flora leads to abnormal immune responses, including loss of T-helper 1 (Th1) cell function. Restoration of the microbiome can reestablish normal Treg and helper cell ratios. Breakdowns in the gut barrier may trigger autoimmune diseases like Crohn's disease by causing inappropriate immune responses to commensal antigens.
keyPoints: Normal gut flora induces anti-inflammatory events via TLRs and PRR signaling.
Loss of Th1 function occurs when normal gut flora is depleted.
Restoration of microbiome can reestablish normal Treg and helper cell ratios.
Breakdown in the gut barrier may contribute to Crohn's disease through immune responses to commensal antigens.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | THE CELLULAR AND MOLECULAR CONTROL OF PROGRAMMED CELL DEATH

items.subtopic: IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | THE CELLULAR AND MOLECULAR CONTROL OF PROGRAMMED CELL DEATH
items.title: Role of Apoptosis in Immune Regulation and Disease
items.content: Apoptosis is essential for regulating immune responses by eliminating infected, damaged, or unnecessary cells. The TNF receptor family plays a key role in apoptosis through signaling cascades that activate caspases, leading to cell death. Mutations in genes involved in apoptotic pathways can lead to autoimmune syndromes and malignancies.
keyPoints: Apoptosis regulates immune responses by eliminating infected or damaged cells.
TNF receptor family is crucial for apoptosis signaling.
Mutations in Fas and Fas ligand genes are linked to autoimmune and lymphoproliferation syndromes.
Associations of mutations in apoptotic pathway genes with malignant syndromes.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · IMMUNE TOLERANCE AND AUTOIMMUNITY (part 2) | THE CELLULAR AND MOLECULAR CONTROL OF PROGRAMMED CELL DEATH

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 1)
items.title: Phases of Host Defenses and Potential for Immune-Mediated Damage
items.content: The host immune response to foreign microbes involves five phases: leukocyte migration, innate pathogen recognition, specific antigen recognition by lymphocytes, inflammatory response amplification, and antigen destruction. While these phases generally protect the host, dysfunction can lead to tissue damage or exacerbate disease morbidity, as seen in certain pneumonias.
keyPoints: Five general phases of host defenses: leukocyte migration, innate pathogen recognition, specific antigen recognition by T and B cells, inflammatory response amplification, and antigen destruction.
Dysfunction in immune responses can damage host tissues and contribute to disease morbidity.
Inflammatory responses may cause significant morbidity in diseases like tuberculosis and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 1)
items.title: Lymphocyte-Endothelial Cell Interactions and Lymphocyte Homing
items.content: Lymphocyte circulation between the bloodstream and lymphoid organs is regulated by interactions with endothelial cells. These interactions involve adhesion molecules that undergo conformational changes for ligand binding, crucial for lymphocyte entry into inflamed tissues.
keyPoints: Adhesion molecule expression regulates lymphocyte retention and egress in antigenic sites.
Lymphocyte migration begins with attachment to high endothelial venules (HEVs).
L-selectin mediates initial rolling, while firm adhesion involves CD11b/18 or CD11a/18 upregulation.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 1)
items.title: Stages of Lymphocyte Emigration and Transendothelial Migration
items.content: Lymphocyte emigration from HEVs involves four stages: rolling, firm adhesion, sticking/arrest, and transendothelial migration. The latter stage is regulated by molecules like CD44 and the glycocalyx.
keyPoints: Four stages of lymphocyte emigration: rolling, firm adhesion, sticking/arrest, and transendothelial migration.
L-selectin initiates rolling; cytokines activate firm adhesion via CD11b/18 or CD11a/18.
Transendothelial migration involves CD44 and the HEV glycocalyx.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 1)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 2)
items.title: Role of Adhesion Molecules in Chronic Inflammatory Diseases
items.content: Abnormal induction and expression of adhesion molecules such as MAdCAM-1, GlyCAM-1, L-selectin, and a4 integrin are implicated in chronic inflammatory diseases. These molecules facilitate lymphocyte emigration into tissues, contributing to the pathology seen in conditions like type 1 diabetes mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Graves' disease, multiple sclerosis, Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis.
keyPoints: MAdCAM-1 and GlyCAM-1 are highly expressed on HEVs in inflamed pancreatic islets in type 1 diabetes mellitus models.
Inhibition of L-selectin and a4 integrin function blocks the development of type 1 diabetes mellitus in animal models.
Abnormal induction of adhesion molecules is suggested to play a role in rheumatoid arthritis, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Graves' disease, multiple sclerosis, Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 2)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 2)
items.title: Immune-Complex Formation and Clearance
items.content: The formation of immune complexes between antigen, complement, and antibody is a crucial host defense mechanism. However, if not cleared by the reticuloendothelial system, these complexes can deposit in tissues like blood vessel walls or renal glomeruli, causing vasculitis or glomerulonephritis. Deficiencies in early complement components lead to inefficient clearance of immune complexes, resulting in autoimmune syndromes or infections with encapsulated bacteria such as S. pneumoniae.
keyPoints: Immune complexes can cause host and foreign cell damage if not cleared properly.
Deficiencies in early complement components are linked to autoimmune syndromes and susceptibility to encapsulated bacterial infections like S. pneumoniae.
Late complement component deficiencies increase susceptibility to recurrent Neisseria infections.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 2)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 2)
items.title: Immediate-Type Hypersensitivity Mechanisms
items.content: Helper T cells, particularly TH2-type inducer T cells, drive antiallergen IgE responses by secreting cytokines like IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, and IL-10. A subset of TFH13 cells produces IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, crucial for IgE-mediated anaphylaxis. Mast cells and basophils, armed with cell-bound antiallergen IgE, release mediators upon antigen interaction that cause allergic reactions by increasing vascular permeability, activating inflammatory cells, and modulating mediator release.
keyPoints: TH2-type inducer T cells secrete IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, and IL-10 to drive antiallergen IgE responses.
TFH13 cells produce IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, playing a key role in anaphylaxis.
Mast cell and basophil mediators increase vascular permeability, activate inflammatory cells, and modulate other mediator releases.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 2)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 3)
items.title: Role of Delayed-Type Hypersensitivity in Immune Responses
items.content: Delayed-type hypersensitivity responses are primarily mediated by T cells, particularly those secreting IFN-γ, IL-2, and TNF-α. These responses involve macrophages and may include NK cells, especially following skin contact with immunogens. The process begins with upregulation of endothelial cell adhesion molecules at the site of antigen exposure, facilitating lymphocyte accumulation.
keyPoints: T cells (especially IFN-γ, IL-2, TNF-α-secreting TH1-type helper T cells) and macrophages are central to delayed-type hypersensitivity responses.
NK cells may play a significant role in skin contact-related delayed hypersensitivity.
Endothelial cell adhesion molecule expression is upregulated at the site of antigen exposure.

Diseases involving delayed-type hypersensitivity include fungal infections (e.g., histoplasmosis), mycobacterial infections (e.g., tuberculosis, leprosy), chlamydial infections (e.g., lymphogranuloma venereum), helminth infections (e.g., schistosomiasis), berylliosis, and hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
Delayed-type hypersensitivity is also implicated in autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, temporal arteritis, and granulomatosis with polyangiitis.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 3)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 3)
items.title: Autophagy in Immune Control of Intracellular Pathogens
items.content: Autophagy is a lysosomal degradation pathway that helps cells dispose of intracellular debris and damaged organelles. In the innate immune system, autophagy plays a crucial role in controlling intracellular pathogens like M. tuberculosis by promoting phagosome maturation and enhancing MHC class II antigen presentation to CD4 T cells.
keyPoints: Autophagy involves lysosomal degradation of intracellular debris and damaged organelles.
It is utilized by innate immune cells to control intracellular pathogens such as M. tuberculosis.
Autophagy aids in phagosome maturation and enhances MHC class II antigen presentation to CD4 T cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · MECHANISMS OF IMMUNE-MEDIATED DAMAGE TO MICROBES OR HOST TISSUES (part 3)

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EVALUATION OF IMMUNE FUNCTION | IMMUNOTHERAPY
items.title: Identifying Immune Deficiencies Based on Infection Patterns
items.content: Assessing immune function involves evaluating the four major components: humoral immunity, cell-mediated immunity, phagocytic cells, and complement. Specific infection patterns can indicate particular immune deficiencies.
keyPoints: Defects in cellular immunity often lead to viral, mycobacterial, and fungal infections; AIDS is an extreme example.
Antibody deficiencies typically result in recurrent bacterial infections with organisms like S. pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae.
Disorders of phagocyte function are usually manifested by recurrent skin infections, often due to Staphylococcus aureus.
Deficiencies in early and late complement components are associated with autoimmune phenomena and recurrent Neisseria infections.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CLINICAL EVALUATION OF IMMUNE FUNCTION | IMMUNOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL EVALUATION OF IMMUNE FUNCTION | IMMUNOTHERAPY
items.title: Targeted Immunotherapy Strategies for Immune-Mediated Diseases
items.content: New immunotherapies aim to specifically interrupt pathologic immune responses while preserving nonpathologic ones, using techniques like monoclonal antibodies and cytokine modulation.
keyPoints: Checkpoint inhibitors such as CTLA-4 inhibitors (ipilimumab, tremelimumab) and anti-PD-1 antibodies (nivolumab) reverse CD8 T-cell exhaustion in melanoma and other solid tumors.
Chimeric antigen receptor T cells (CAR T cells), approved by the FDA for certain leukemias and lymphomas, target leukemic cells using engineered autologous T cells.
items.reference: Introduction to the Immune System · CLINICAL EVALUATION OF IMMUNE FUNCTION | IMMUNOTHERAPY

Overreactive autoreactive T and B cells

items.subtopic: THE INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM | PATTERN RECOGNITION
items.question: What is the role of Toll-like receptors (TLRs) upon ligation?
options: They lead to the killing of bacteria- and viral-infected cells, and recruit antigen-specific T and B lymphocytes.
They solely upregulate cell-surface molecules without producing cytokines.
They function exclusively in nonhematopoietic cells.
items.explanation: Upon ligation, TLRs activate a series of intracellular events that lead to the killing of bacteria- and viral-infected cells as well as to the recruitment and ultimate activation of antigen-specific T and B lymphocytes.

options: They mediate signal transduction after binding bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and activate phagocytic cells.
They are involved in the production of cytokines.
items.explanation: Integrins signal after cells bind bacterial LPS and activate phagocytic cells to ingest pathogens, as described in the passage.

Stimulate naïve CD4 T cells to differentiate into peripheral Tregs
